title: Crash in SessionDataTask.mutableData copy (Data(_mutableData) → __DataStorage.init)   on iOS 26.5 — regression surfaced by the #2524 fix
author: MANEG-SEON-A
body:
## Summary

  After upgrading to **8.10.0** (which includes #2524, "Fix SessionDataTask mutable data COW sharing", the fix for #2523), we are seeing a **new** production crash on  **iOS 26.5** in the very copy that #2524 introduced.

  #2524 changed the getter to:

  ```swift
  public var mutableData: Data {
      lock.lock()
      defer { lock.unlock() }
      return Data(_mutableData)   // SessionDataTask.swift:48
  }
  ```

  The trap in #2523 was on the **write side** (`Data.append` → `_DataStorage.ensureUniqueBufferReference`). With 8.10.0 it has **moved to the copy side**: constructing `Data(_mutableData)` now traps inside Foundation's  `__DataStorage.init(bytes:length:)`. So the same iOS-26 `SessionDataTask`  instability appears to remain, just at a different site.

  ## Environment

  - **Kingfisher**: 8.10.0
  - **OS**: iOS 26.5.0 only. No events on earlier iOS.
  - **Devices**: iPhone 14, iPhone 15 (not device-specific)
  - **Free memory at crash**: 67 MB and 370 MB on two separate events — **not memory-pressure dependent**.
  - **Usage**: standard `kf.setImage` with `DownsamplingImageProcessor`. **No  progressive decoding** option. Only customizations:   `ImageDownloader.default.downloadTimeout = 20`, `ImageCache` size limits. No custom  `ImageDownloaderDelegate`.
  - Source: production Crashlytics.

  ## Stack trace (representative)

  ```
  Crashed: com.apple.NSURLSession-delegate
  EXC_BREAKPOINT

  0   Foundation      __DataStorage.init(bytes:length:) + 300
  2   App             closure #1 in Data.init<A>(_:)
  3   App             SessionDataTask.swift:48   closure #6 in ImageDownloader.setupSessionHandler()
  4   App             Delegate.swift:91          closure #1 in  Delegate.delegate<A>(on:block:)
  5   App             Delegate.swift:141         Delegate<>.call(_:)
  6   App             SessionDelegate.swift:218  SessionDelegate.urlSession(_:task:didCompleteWithError:)
  7   App            @objc SessionDelegate.urlSession(_:task:didCompleteWithError:)
  8   CFNetwork      __79-[__NSCFURLSessionDelegateWrapper task:didCompleteWithError:completionHandler:]_block_invoke + 36
  ...
  ```

  Path: `urlSession(_:task:didCompleteWithError:)` (success branch) →
  `onDidDownloadData.call(sessionTask)` →
   the `onDidDownloadData` closure in
  `setupSessionHandler` reads `task.mutableData` →
   the `mutableData` getter
  (`Data(_mutableData)`, inlined) → 
  generic `Data.init<S: Sequence>` →
  `__DataStorage.init(bytes:length:)` → 
  trap.

  ## Before/after across our app versions (Crashlytics)

  Filtering Crashlytics by `SessionDataTask` shows **two distinct issues**, and the
  split lines up exactly with our Kingfisher upgrade:

  | Issue | Site | App versions | Kingfisher |
  |---|---|---|---|
  | **A** (pre-existing) | `SessionDelegate.urlSession(_:dataTask:didReceive:)` —
  append/receive side | older → current | 8.4.x and earlier, continuing |
  | **B** (this report, NEW) | `Data(_mutableData)` copy in `mutableData` getter,
  `SessionDataTask.swift:48` | current build only | **8.10.0** |

 Issue B appeared **only** in the build where we adopted 8.10.0 (which contains #2524), while the older append-side crash (A) has been present for many versions.

 Happy to provide additional Crashlytics samples, or to test a candidate fix against our production traffic.
 
 additional Crashlytics samples if useful.
 Related: #2523, #2524, #2539, #2541.

title: Race in `SessionDataTask.mutableData`: COW buffer leak causes `assertionFailure` in `Data.append` on iOS 26
author: jeongju9216
body:
## Summary

`SessionDataTask.didReceiveData(_:)` traps in `_DataStorage.ensureUniqueBufferReference` on iOS 26.x. The `mutableData` public getter returns `_mutableData` directly under the lock, which (due to `Data`'s COW semantics) returns a reference to the caller that shares the same `_DataStorage`. While that shared reference is alive, the next `_mutableData.append(data)` inside the lock sees a non-unique buffer and traps.

Commit `aa5996c2` ("Fix a racing on data reading in session", 2024-12-01) added mutual exclusion but the COW sharing through the getter remains, so the race appears to be reduced rather than eliminated. iOS 26 makes it consistently observable in production.

## Environment

- Kingfisher: 8.7.0, 8.8.0, 8.8.1 — all reproduce the same call stack
- OS: iOS / iPadOS 26.x only (26.3.1 / 26.4.x / 26.5)
- Devices: broad coverage across recent iPhones and iPads. Not device-specific.
- Network: Wi-Fi and Cellular both reproduce.
- Free memory at crash: 26 MB to 2.7 GB. Not memory-pressure dependent.

## Stack Trace (representative)

```
0  libswiftCore.dylib   assertionFailure(_:_:file:line:flags:)
1  Foundation           _DataStorage.ensureUniqueBufferReference(growingTo:clear:)
2  Foundation           _DataStorage.replaceBytes(in:with:length:)
3  Foundation           Data.InlineSlice.append(contentsOf:)
4  Foundation           Data._Representation.append(contentsOf:)
5  Foundation           Data.append(_:)
6  <App>                SessionDelegate.urlSession(_:dataTask:didReceive:)  (SessionDelegate.swift:188)
7  CFNetwork            __NSCFURLSessionDelegateWrapper dataTask:didReceiveData:completionHandler:
8  libdispatch          _dispatch_workloop_worker_thread
```

A second variant traps at `SessionDelegate.swift:257` (the `redirectHandler.handleHTTPRedirection` site) at the same trap location, suggesting the same race surfaces along multiple paths into `SessionDataTask`.

## Root Cause

`Sources/Networking/SessionDataTask.swift` (8.8.1):

```swift
private var _mutableData: Data
public var mutableData: Data {
    lock.lock()
    defer { lock.unlock() }
    return _mutableData              // returns a COW share of _DataStorage
}

func didReceiveData(_ data: Data) {
    lock.lock()
    defer { lock.unlock() }
    _mutableData.append(data)        // traps when _DataStorage refcount > 1
}
```

The lock guarantees mutual exclusion, but the `mutableData` getter hands back a `Data` value that shares the same `_DataStorage` as `_mutableData` via COW. While the returned value is alive, the next `didReceiveData(_:)` enters the lock with refcount > 1, and `append` traps inside `ensureUniqueBufferReference`.

The getter is called from three internal Kingfisher sites, so library users cannot avoid this from the outside:

- `Sources/Networking/ImageDownloader.swift:301` — called on every completed download
- `Sources/Image/ImageProgressive.swift:126` — progressive decoding
- `Sources/General/KingfisherOptionsInfo.swift:511` — download progress

## Distribution

Sampled over 14 days from a single production iOS app (sampling cap of 100, so true volume is higher):

- **OS**: iOS / iPadOS 26.x — 100%. Zero events on iOS 25 or earlier.
- **Hashkeys**: 6 distinct StackTraceHashkeys collapse to the same entry point and trap site, two of which account for ~89%.
- **Trend**: increasing. Roughly 280 cumulative events / 100+ unique users over the window.

The OS-only distribution suggests that the race has been latent for some time, and that iOS 26 now exposes it consistently — possibly via a stricter uniqueness check on the `Data` mutation path, though I haven't found a public release note confirming the exact change.

## Related

- #1811, #1699, #1587, #1277, #1324 — same trap signature, previously attributed to memory pressure or progressive JPEG. The data above (free memory 26 MB – 2.7 GB, no progressive option required) suggests a different underlying cause.

## Proposed Fix

Break the COW share in the getter:

```swift
public var mutableData: Data {
    lock.lock()
    defer { lock.unlock() }
    return Data(_mutableData)        // force a copy so the caller does not share _DataStorage
}
```

This keeps `_mutableData` uniquely referenced regardless of how long the caller retains the returned value. The copy cost is bounded by the in-flight buffer size and is paid only at the three internal call sites above. The public API is unchanged.

title: I am facing  error in new version 8.8.0 Unable to find module dependency: 'TVUIKit'

title: ImageCache.imageCachedType performs synchronous stat calls on caller thread, causing main-thread hangs under disk pressure
author: aliakhtar-hu
body:
We're seeing production hangs. Representative stack trace (all frames on main thread):

`ImageCache.imageCachedType(forKey:processorIdentifier:) `calls into `DiskStorage.Backend.isCached`, which in turn calls `DiskStorage.Backend.value(forKey:...) `

swift// DiskStorage.swift
```
guard fileManager.fileExists(atPath: filePath) else { return nil }           // stat #1
let meta = try FileMeta(fileURL: fileURL, resourceKeys: [...])               // stat #2 (resource values)
```
Because `KingfisherManager.retrieveImage → retrieveImageFromCache → imageCachedType` runs synchronously on whatever queue called `KingfisherWrapper.setImage`, these stat calls land on the main thread whenever setImage is called from cellForRowAtIndexPath, collectionView(_:cellForItemAt:), or any other UIKit layout callback 

```
libsystem_kernel    stat
Foundation          _FileManagerImpl._fileExists
Foundation          _NSFileManagerBridge.fileExists
Kingfisher          DiskStorage.Backend.value (DiskStorage.swift:260)
Kingfisher          DiskStorage.Backend.isCached (DiskStorage.swift:318)
Kingfisher          DiskStorage.Backend.isCached (DiskStorage.swift:301)
Kingfisher          ImageCache.imageCachedType (ImageCache.swift:924)
Kingfisher          KingfisherManager.retrieveImageFromCache (KingfisherManager.swift:594)
Kingfisher          KingfisherManager.retrieveImage (KingfisherManager.swift:400)
Kingfisher          KingfisherManager.retrieveImage (KingfisherManager.swift:377)
Kingfisher          KingfisherWrapper<T>.setImage (ImageView+Kingfisher.swift:320)
Kingfisher          KingfisherWrapper<T>.setImage (ImageView+Kingfisher.swift:86)
Kingfisher          KingfisherWrapper<T>.setImage (ImageView+Kingfisher.swift:173)
<App>               UIImageView.setKFImage (wrapper)
<App>               TileContainer.set
<App>               TableViewCell.updateTiles (forEach over tiles)
<App>               TableViewCell.set
UIKitCore           -[__UIDiffableDataSource tableView:cellForRowAtIndexPath:]
UIKitCore           -[UITableView _createPreparedCellForGlobalRow:...]
UIKitCore           -[UITableView _heightForRowAtIndexPath:]
UIKitCore           -[_UITableViewUpdateSupport _faultInRealHeightsOfNeededElements]
UIKitCore           -[UITableView _endCellAnimationsWithContext:]
```

Kingfisher 8.5.0

title: Images loaded with ImageDataProvider cannot be cancelled
author: FaizanDurrani
body:
Images being loaded asynchronously using an ImageDataProvider cannot be cancelled via `imageView.kf.cancelDownloadTask()`. Currently an alternative to this is to implement a custom cancel method on the ImageDataProvider and store a reference to it in the view that is using it and call `dataProvider.cancel()` on it instead. 

This is a lot more verbose and error prone as someone who might be used to using cancelDownloadTask() may forget to explicitly call cancel on the image data provider (or if the view's image loading logic is abstracted out then it's difficult to support cancelling without introducing something like CancellableImageDataProvider which is a wrapper on ImageDataProvider).

An example usecase of this is implementing some custom protocol based image loading from network that may not be supported by default by Kingfisher.

title: How to respect cache-control response headers?
author: buscarini
body: From what I'm seeing, it seems that Kingfisher just ignores the response cache-control headers, which is weird. How can I respect the max-age field so that some images refresh more often? It seems that Kingfisher is caching images forever, but some images need to be refreshed quite often.

title: New App Hangs since updating from 8.6.0 to 8.8.0
author: hotngui
body:
We are seeing more crashes in our apps since we updated to Kingfisher 8.8.0 recently - that release has a fix we needed. Like most App Hang crashes the Watchdog does not allow crash loggers (like Sentry) to have access to any real data so it's hard to tell what's really going on.

I was wondering if you've heard of others seeing an increase in hangs.

title: Vulnerability in Ruby JSON
author: mmoser-hw
body:
Hello,

As stated in this [CVE](https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-33210), this repository at the time of the latest release (8.8.0) uses a vulnerable version of Ruby JSON (2.18.1 in Gemfile.lock). I've seen that you since updated this library, but the last release build of Kingfisher still uses the unsafe version and it triggers our dependancy analysis before production builds. Would it be possible to release a new one with the latest dependency ? 

Thanks in advance !
Meven

title: Refactor TaskPropertyAccessor to absorb cancellation token lifecycle
author: onevcat
body:
## Summary

`KingfisherWrapper.setImage(...)` currently passes `previousCancellationToken` and `setCancellationToken` as standalone parameters in addition to `TaskPropertyAccessor`.

These token-related parameters belong to the same task lifecycle concern as:
- `setTaskIdentifier`
- `getTaskIdentifier`
- `setTask`

The current shape works, but it creates parameter sprawl and makes the helper signature harder to read.

## Proposed Direction

Fold cancellation-token access into `TaskPropertyAccessor` so the helper receives a single lifecycle accessor instead of splitting task state across:
- `taskAccessor`
- `previousCancellationToken`
- `setCancellationToken`

Possible shape:
- add token getter/setter accessors to `TaskPropertyAccessor`
- let the helper cancel the previous token and store the new token through that accessor

## Scope

This is a readability / maintainability cleanup only.
It should not change behavior.

## Non-Goals

- do not change the current stale-disk-cache semantics
- do not refactor unrelated associated-object patterns in this issue
- do not change public API surface

## Affected Files

Likely at least:
- `Sources/Extensions/HasImageComponent+Kingfisher.swift`
- `Sources/Extensions/ImageView+Kingfisher.swift`
- `Sources/Extensions/NSTextAttachment+Kingfisher.swift`
- `Sources/Extensions/CPListItem+Kingfisher.swift`
- `Sources/Extensions/UIButton+Kingfisher.swift`
- `Sources/Extensions/NSButton+Kingfisher.swift`

## Acceptance

- helper signature becomes simpler
- token lifecycle stays encapsulated with other task lifecycle accessors
- no behavior change
- existing stale cache tests continue to pass

title: Images loading from cache are not canceled
author: FaizanDurrani
body: Scrolling through a large collection of images quickly explodes the memory usage (>1gb). I believe its because cached images go through deserialisation even if the download task is canceled using `imageView.kf.cancelDownloadTask()`. I am using yeatse/KingfisherWebP package and can see `ImageCache.retrieveImageInDiskCache` being called for each displayed item.

title: WebP animation starts after ~5 seconds delay in Debug build
author: LEEbyeonghoon2
body:
Hello,

We are currently using a WebP animated image on the intro screen of our app.

The WebP file is stored on disk. We read the file data and create a RawImageDataProvider from that data, which is then passed to setImage.

In the Release build, the WebP animation starts immediately as expected.
**However, in the Debug build, the animation starts only after a delay of about 5 seconds.**

We verified that the setImage completionHandler is called with .success,
so it appears that the image loading itself completes successfully.

We are using AnimatedImageView, and framePreloadCount is set to 4.

The KingfisherOptionsInfo options we are using are:

`.transition(.fade(0.2)),
.processor(WebPProcessor.default),
.cacheSerializer(WebPSerializer.default),
.cacheMemoryOnly`

WebP file information:
- File size: 1.92MB
- Resolution: 1920 × 1920

Version information:
- Kingfisher: 8.0.0
- KingfisherWebP: 1.7.0

We would like to understand why this delay occurs only in Debug builds,
and whether there is a way to make the animation start immediately in Debug mode as well.

Any guidance would be appreciated.
Thank you.
